text: This is a video of a total laparoscopic hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with cystoscopy.
The surgical team utilizes a blunt probe, harmonic shears, a bipolar vessel sealing device, and laparoscopic needle drivers during the laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon inspects the pelvic region after gaining access to the abdominal cavity, visually assessing the anatomical structures within the surgical field.

The surgeon systematically inspects and manipulates the anatomical structures, starting anteriorly, moving to the left adnexa, then to the right adnexa, and finishing in the posterior cul-de-sac during the total laparoscopic hysterectomy.

The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric artery on the right side, with an arrow indicating the pulsating vessel. The artery is visible exiting the inguinal canal, positioned medial to the insertion of the round ligament on the pelvic sidewall.
The surgeon injects a solution of quarter percent Marcaine into the trocar sites prior to insertion, aiming to reduce postoperative pain.
The surgical setup includes four 5mm trocar sites: one for the camera through the umbilicus, two in the bilateral lower quadrants, and one placed suprapubically.
With all trocharsites established, the surgeon uses blunt probes to identify the appendix within the abdominal cavity.
The arrowhead points to the ureter as it is identified transperitoneally on the right pelvic sidewall.
The surgeon identifies the colpotomy cup, followed by pointing out the utero-sacral ligaments within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon moves to the left pelvic sidewall and visually identifies the ureter transperitoneally.
The surgeon points out the right-sided round ligament and fallopian tube, followed by identification of the left round ligament, fallopian tube, and ovary.
The surgeon begins the hysterectomy procedure by transecting the right round ligament using a harmonic scalpel, with the ligament visibly being cut and separated.

The surgeon extends the dissection of the right round ligament to the peritoneum, visually identifying the ureter on the right side. The right infundibulopelvic ligament is isolated to prevent ureteral damage during its transection.
The surgeon uses traction-countertraction to initiate the dissection of the peritoneum, visibly manipulating the tissue layers to separate them.
The surgeon extends the peritoneal dissection parallel to the infundibulopelvic ligament, carefully avoiding accidental injury and excessive hemorrhage.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection within the peritoneal space to expose the iliac vessels and identify the ureter, ensuring clear visualization of these anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies the ureter by observing its vermiculation and proceeds to create a window in the peritoneum on the sidewall.
The surgeon creates a window through the peritoneum after safely identifying the ureter, isolating the infundibulopelvic ligament to ensure it can be taken without compromising the ureter.
It's important to always again spread parallel to the IP to not cause excessive bleeding.

The surgeon prepares to transect the infundibulopelvic ligament at a point proximal to the ovary, ensuring that the ovarian tissue, located approximately two centimeters proximal to the ovary, is preserved.
The surgeon inspects the infundibulopelvic ligament after it has been taken, ensuring good hemostasis with no visible bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the colpotomy cup positioned anteriorly and indicates the area where the bladder flap will be directed.
The surgeon develops the bladder flap by dissecting the anterior leaf of the broad ligament laterally using a combination of blunt and sharp techniques, revealing the underlying tissue layers.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to achieve hemostasis, addressing any oozing to maintain a clear operative field during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon develops the bladder flap to safely retract the bladder away from the operative field, minimizing the risk of injury, while identifying the pubic cervical fascia.
The surgeon shifts focus from the anterior dissection to the posterior aspect, identifying the colpotomy ring and proceeding to dissect the posterior leaf of the broad ligament.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the posterior leaf of the broad ligament and proceeds to skeletonize the uterine vessels, enhancing their exposure and facilitating hemostasis.
The surgeon identifies the colpotomy ring and prepares to ligate the uterine vessels, ensuring to stay medial to the ring to minimize the risk of ureteral injury.
The surgeon ligates the uterine vessels while staying medial to the colpotomy ring to minimize the risk of ureteral injury, a common concern at this site.
The surgeon coagulates the right uterine vessels and then shifts focus to the left round ligament, initiating dissection on the opposite side.

It's important to remember when operating laparoscopically and using a four-port configuration to use your suprapubic hand to help provide traction and counter-traction, especially when dissecting the retroperitoneal space. Opening up this space will allow the pneumoperitoneum to enter retroperitoneally to expose the tissue planes necessary to perform your dissection.
The surgeon encounters oozing in the retroperitoneal space and uses a suction tip to clear the blood, improving visibility of the surgical field.

The surgeon develops the pararectal space, with the round ligament forming the superior border, the external iliac artery as the lateral border, and the ureter as the medial border. The dissection is kept medial as the ureter passes on the medial leaf of the broad ligament beneath the uterine vessel.
Due to bleeding in the left-sided retroperitoneal space, the surgeon transitions to a transperitoneal approach to visualize the ureter, aiming to locate its course within the surgical field.
In the bloody operative field, the surgeon identifies the left ureter, visible amidst the surrounding tissue and blood.
The surgeon creates a window in the left retroperitoneal space, spreading parallel to the infundibulopelvic ligament to minimize bleeding risk.
The surgeon coagulates the infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament proximal to the ovary, ligates it, and inspects the IP stump to ensure hemostasis, checking for any signs of bleeding.

The surgeon develops the anterior leaf of the broad ligament on the patient's left side, connecting it to the previously created bladder flap. Subsequently, the posterior leaf of the broad ligament is dissected on the left side, joining the dissection completed on the right side.
The surgeon skeletonizes the left uterine vessels, carefully isolating them to improve hemostasis using a bipolar vessel sealing device.
The surgeon skeletonizes the uterine vessels and cauterizes them, while the assistant pushes the uterus cephalad with significant force using the uterine manipulator.
The surgeon elevates the uterine vessels away from the ureter, ensuring they are not accidentally clamped or incorporated into the cauterized area, minimizing thermal spread during the procedure.

The surgeon uses harmonic shears to transect the patient's left uterine vessel, following coagulation. The cuts are made in an inverted C shape, allowing the pedicle of the uterine vessel to fall away from the specimen.
The surgeon moves to the patient's right side to secure the uterine vessels, mirroring the steps previously completed on the left side, including transection and coagulation.

The surgeon uses harmonic shears to make cuts in an inverted C shape, allowing the uterine pedicle to fall away from the specimen. If bleeding occurs, the surgeon replaces the shears with a bipolar device to cauterize the uterine vessels for complete sealing.
The uterus appears blanched, indicating that both uterine vessels have been successfully secured and coagulated.

After securing both uterine vessels, the surgeon palpates the colpotomy cup and begins the anterior incision using harmonic shears. The incision is made with various techniques, including drilling with the active blade of the harmonic device.
The surgeon takes small bites of the thicker tissue above the colpotomy ring to thin it out before proceeding with the drilling technique on the colpotomy cup.
The surgeon uses harmonic shears to perform a circumferential dissection around the colpotomy cup, ensuring the dissection remains medial to the uterine pedicles to prevent unnecessary bleeding.

The surgeon demonstrates the colpotomy cup, marked by a green arrow, showing its two lips: one near the specimen and one near the vaginal tissue. The incision is made on the inner lip to preserve vaginal tissue for re-approximation of the vaginal cuff post-specimen removal.

The uterus, having been completely severed, is removed through the vagina with assistance from below and gentle pushing from above. The specimen removal process is visually challenging, particularly with large specimens, and may involve techniques such as bivalving the uterus and vaginal morcellation to facilitate tissue extraction.

A vertical incision is made in the posterior cuff of the vagina to create additional space for specimen removal. After the specimen is removed, the surgeon inspects the vaginal cuff to ensure it is hemostatic before beginning closure.

The laparoscopic setup includes four 5mm trocars. An ovicral suture on a CT2 needle is back-loaded through one of the lower quadrant ports to fit into the abdominal cavity, as the needle is too large for a 5mm trocar.

The surgeon closes the vaginal cuff using an ovicral suture in a running fashion, performing intracorporeal knot tying. The suture is carefully placed to include sufficient vaginal mucosa, ensuring the prevention of cuff dehiscence.

Total laparoscopic hysterectomy has been found to have a higher incidence of vaginal cuff dehiscence when compared to either total abdominal, laparoscopic assisted vaginal, or total vaginal hysterectomy. Vaginal cuff closure is the third most common place for ureteric injury in a laparoscopic hysterectomy.

It's important to remember that when you're closing the vaginal cuff angles, you don't want to place your stitches too far lateral into the sidewall as you run the risk of either ligating or kinking the ureters.

The surgeon closes the vaginal cuff using a running suture technique. After taking bites and pulling the needle through, the surgeon drops the needle and uses the suture alone to avoid danger in the abdominal cavity. The hand over hand technique is demonstrated to pull the suture through, with needle drivers kept to the right of the suture to prevent accidental locking.

The surgeon demonstrates a technique for loading the needle using self-righting needle drivers. The needle is placed on a flat surface, pressed down, and locked into the needle driver for proper loading.

The surgeon demonstrates taking posterior bites during vaginal cuff closure, ensuring incorporation of the peritoneum into each suture to achieve hemostasis and prevent bleeding from this vascular structure.
The surgeon demonstrates intracorporeal knot tying, using a suprapubic needle driver to grasp further away and increase suture length, facilitating easier knot tying within the abdominal cavity.
After closing the vaginal cuff and removing the suture, the surgeon identifies the ureter transperitoneally, observing good vermiculation on both sides.

The surgeon irrigates the pelvis thoroughly to ensure hemostasis from all pedicles and the vaginal cuff. The scene then transitions to a cystoscopic view inside the bladder, showing the internal bladder lining.

The surgeon orients the view by identifying an air bubble at the dome of the bladder. The camera then focuses on the trigone area to locate the patient's left ureteral orifice (UO), observing efflux. The view shifts to the right ureteral orifice, where efflux is also noted bilaterally.

The surgeon performs a systematic survey of the bladder, visually inspecting for any signs of injury, stones, or masses. The view then shifts to an overhead perspective to confirm adequate hemostasis before concluding the procedure.
